(2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA570&dq= "Kuge"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 570.</ref> The ''kuge'' were important from the establishment of Kyoto as the capital during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} in the late 8th century until the rise of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}} in the 12th century, at which point it was eclipsed by the [[:en:bushi (warrior)|bushi]]. The ''kuge'' still provided a weak court around the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}} until the [[Meiji Restoration]], when they merged with the daimyō, regaining some of their status in the process, and formed the [[kazoku]] ({{ill|Peerage|en|Peerage|WD=Q51280089|lt=Peerage|1=Peerage}}), which lasted until shortly after {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} (1947), when the Japanese peerage system was abolished. Though there is no longer an official status, members of the [[:en:List of Kuge families|kuge families]] remain influential in Japanese society, government, and industry.<ref>Lebra, ''Above the Clouds: Status Culture of the Modern Japanese Nobility''.</ref>
==History== ''Kuge'' (from [[Mi11le Chinese]] ''kuwng-kæ'' 公家, "ducal family", or "nobility" in a pre–[[Kazoku|peerage context]]) originally described the Emperor and his court. The meaning of the word changed over time to designate {{ill|Bureaucrat|en|Bureaucrat|WD=Q572700|lt=Bureaucrat|1=Bureaucrat}}s at the court. During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, the relative peace and stability provided freedom for the noble class to pursue cultural interests, and the kuge became leaders and benefactors of arts and culture in Japan.<ref>Lorraine Witt, [http://academic.mu.edu/meissnerd/witt.html "Poetry and Processions: The Daily Life of the Kuge in the Heian Court"], accessed 30/4/2012</ref> Most of the Kuge resided in the capital city of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}.<ref>John Whitney Hall, Jeffrey P. Mass, [https://books.google.com/books?id=J-tO9pOjzmsC&pg=PA3 "Medieval Japan: Essays in Institutional History"] Stanford University Press, 1988, accessed 30/4/2012</ref>
Later in the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} (1185–1333), ''kuge'' became an antonym to ''{{ill|Buke (Japan)|en|Buke (Japan)|WD=Q2927809|lt=buke|1=Buke}}'' (warrior nobles), that is, samurai who swore loyalty to the shogunate. At this point, ''kuge'' began to be used to describe those who worked in the Court; both aristocratic noblemen and commoners.
Two classes formed the kuge: the {{nihongo||堂上|dōjō}} noblemen who sat on the floor with the Emperor; and the {{nihongo||地下|jige}} who were unable to sit with the Emperor. Although kuge included those two classes, mainly this word described the dōjō, the noblemen.
The highest offices at the court were called ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō|1=Kugyō}}'' and eligibility was limited to members of dōjō kuge. During the Edo period there were about 130 families of dōjō kuge. The most prominent members of the ''kuge'' became regents to the emperor (''[[:en:Sesshō|Sesshō]] or [[:en:Kampaku|Kampaku]]''). These ''[[daijō-kan]]'' offices were restricted to members of the [[:en:Fujiwara family|Fujiwara family]].
Though they lost most of their political power, they sustained the court culture and maintained a cultural influence. In particular, after the [[:en:Sengoku|Sengoku]] period they lost most of their financial basis and were no longer in a position to act as patrons of culture, but they passed on their knowledge as masters of particular fields such as writing {{ill|Waka (poetry)|en|Waka (poetry)|WD=Q737148|lt=waka|1=Waka (poetry)}} poetry and playing instruments such as the {{ill|Biwa|en|Biwa|WD=Q829729|lt=Biwa|1=Biwa}}, and they had disciples among the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' and sometimes rich commoners. As masters of a certain field, kuge gave their disciples many licenses certifying that the disciples had learned a certain field and allowed them to perform in public or sometimes to teach others. Disciples were expected to pay their master a fee for each issued license. During the Edo period, this was an important source of income for the ''kuge''.
In 1869 during the [[Meiji Restoration]] the ''kuge'' merged with the daimyō to form a single aristocratic group, the ''[[kazoku]]''.
Others associated with the ''kuge'' included {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} priests, Kyoto cultural patrons, {{ill|Geisha|en|Geisha|WD=Q82723|lt=Geisha|1=Geisha}}, and actors.
==Classification== The ''kuge'' were divided into two classes, the higher ''dojo'' and the lower ''jige''. In the 12th century conventional differences were established among the ''dōjō'', separating them into groups according to their office at court. These determined the highest office to which they could be appointed. Within the ''dojo'' class, the groupings were: #{{nihongo||摂家|1=Five regent houses|1=Five regent houses|'''Sekke'''}}: could be appointed as [[Sesshō and Kampaku]]: This was the highest class of kuge. Only five families belonged to this class, all descended from {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|en|Fujiwara no Michinaga|WD=Q433133|lt=Fujiwara no Michinaga|1=Fujiwara no Michinaga}}. #{{nihongo||清華家|1=Seiga families|2=ja|3=清華家|'''Seiga families'''}}: could be appointed ''[[daijin]]'' (minister), including ''{{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Daijō-daijin|1=Daijō-daijin}}'' (chancellor), the highest of the four ministers of the court. They were descended from the [[Fujiwara clan]] or [[Minamoto clan]], descendants of the emperors. #{{nihongo||大臣家|1=Daijinke|2=ja|3=大臣家|'''Daijinke'''}}: could be appointed ''{{ill|Naidaijin|en|Naidaijin|WD=Q1185338|lt=Naidaijin|1=Naidaijin}}'', if this office became vacant. In reality, the highest office they could normally achieve was ''{{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon|1=Dainagon}}''. #{{nihongo||羽林家|1=Baronial Houses|1=Baronial Houses|2=ja|3=羽林家|'''Urinke'''}}: was a military class; they could be appointed ''dainagon'' or rarely to ''naidaijin''. #{{nihongo||名家|1=Mei families|2=ja|3=名家 (公家)|'''Meika'''|also pronounced "''Meike''"}}: was a civilian class; they could also be appointed ''dainagon''. #{{nihongo||半家|1=Half-house (Kuge)|2=ja|3=半家 (公家)|'''Hanke'''}}: was the lowest class among the dōjō, created in the late {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}. They could only be appointed to lower Ranks than ''{{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|WD=Q1133663|lt=sangi|1=Sangi (Japan)}}'' or ''{{ill|Chūnagon|en|Chūnagon|WD=Q1185382|lt=Chūnagon|1=Chūnagon}}''.
The ''jige'' class was associated with but not a part of the ''dojo'': *{{nihongo||地下家|1=Jigeke|2=ja|3=地下家|lt=Jigeke|'''Jigeke'''}}:This class is not a part of all the classes above. Their status is lower than Hanke and they may not enter certain important places in the imperial palace. They are in charge of miscellaneous business in the palace such as cooking and sanitary services. Like other Kuge classes, their positions and Ranks in the imperial court are hereditary. Most of the highest-classed kuge belonged to the [[Fujiwara clan]] and [[Minamoto clan]], but there were still other clans like the [[Sugawara clan]], the {{ill|Kiyohara clan|en|Kiyohara clan|WD=Q2975295|lt=Kiyohara clan|1=Kiyohara clan}}, and the [[Ōe clan]].
==See also== *[[:en:List of Kuge families|List of Kuge families]]

| languages = | philosophies = | religions = | related_groups = | footnotes = }} {{Italic title|reason= }} The {{nihongo||公家|1=Kuge|'''kuge'''}} was a Japanese {{ill|Aristocracy (class)|en|Aristocracy (class)|qid=Q2478141|lt=aristocratic|1=Aristocracy (class)}} {{ill|Social class|en|Social class|qid=Q187588|lt=class|1=Social class}} that dominated the Japanese {{ill|Imperial Court in Kyoto|en|Imperial Court in Kyoto|qid=Q11680900|lt=Imperial Court in Kyoto|1=Imperial Court in Kyoto}}.<ref>{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|qid=Q726040|lt=Louis Frédéric|1=Louis Frédéric}}. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA570&dq= "Kuge"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 570.</ref> The ''kuge'' were important from the establishment of Kyoto as the capital during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} in the late 8th century until the rise of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|qid=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}} in the 12th century, at which point it was eclipsed by the [[:en:bushi (warrior)|bushi]]. Though there is no longer an official status, members of the [[:en:List of Kuge families|kuge families]] remain influential in Japanese society, government, and industry.<ref>Lebra, ''Above the Clouds: Status Culture of the Modern Japanese Nobility''.</ref>

Mass, [https://books.google.com/books?id=J-tO9pOjzmsC&pg=PA3 "Medieval Japan: Essays in Institutional History"] Stanford University Press, 1988, accessed 30/4/2012</ref>

Only five families belonged to this class, all descended from {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|en|Fujiwara no Michinaga|qid=Q433133|lt=Fujiwara no Michinaga|1=Fujiwara no Michinaga}}. #{{nihongo||清華家|1=Seiga families|2=ja|3=清華家|'''Seiga families'''}}: could be appointed ''[[daijin]]'' (minister), including ''{{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|qid=Q1157586|lt=Daijō-daijin|1=Daijō-daijin}}'' (chancellor), the highest of the four ministers of the court. They were descended from the [[Fujiwara clan]] or [[Minamoto clan]], descendants of the emperors. #{{nihongo||大臣家|1=Daijinke|2=ja|3=大臣家|'''Daijinke'''}}: could be appointed ''{{ill|Naidaijin|en|Naidaijin|qid=Q1185338|lt=Naidaijin|1=Naidaijin}}'', if this office became vacant. In reality, the highest office they could normally achieve was ''{{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|qid=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon|1=Dainagon}}''. #{{nihongo||羽林家|1=Baronial Houses|1=Baronial Houses|2=ja|3=羽林家|'''Urinke'''}}: was a military class; they could be appointed ''dainagon'' or rarely to ''naidaijin''. #{{nihongo||名家|1=Mei families|2=ja|3=名家 (公家)|'''Meika'''|also pronounced "''Meike''"}}: was a civilian class; they could also be appointed ''dainagon''. #{{nihongo||半家|1=Half-house (Kuge)|2=ja|3=半家 (公家)|'''Hanke'''}}: was the lowest class among the dōjō, created in the late {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}. They could only be appointed to lower Ranks than ''{{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|qid=Q1133663|lt=sangi|1=Sangi (Japan)}}'' or ''{{ill|Chūnagon|en|Chūnagon|qid=Q1185382|lt=Chūnagon|1=Chūnagon}}''.

Two classes formed the kuge: the {{nihongo||堂上|dōjō}} noblemen who sat on the floor with the Emperor; and the {{nihongo||地下|jige}} who were unable to sit with the Emperor. Although kuge included those two classes, mainly this word described the dōjō, the noblemen.
The highest offices at the court were called ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|qid=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō|1=Kugyō}}'' and eligibility was limited to members of dōjō kuge. During the Edo period there were about 130 families of dōjō kuge. The most prominent members of the ''kuge'' became regents to the emperor (''[[:en:Sesshō|Sesshō]] or [[:en:Kampaku|Kampaku]]''). These ''[[daijō-kan]]'' offices were restricted to members of the [[:en:Fujiwara family|Fujiwara family]].
Though they lost most of their political power, they sustained the court culture and maintained a cultural influence. In particular, after the [[:en:Sengoku|Sengoku]] period they lost most of their financial basis and were no longer in a position to act as patrons of culture, but they passed on their knowledge as masters of particular fields such as writing {{ill|Waka (poetry)|en|Waka (poetry)|qid=Q737148|lt=waka|1=Waka (poetry)}} poetry and playing instruments such as the {{ill|Biwa|en|Biwa|qid=Q829729|lt=Biwa|1=Biwa}}, and they had disciples among the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' and sometimes rich commoners. As masters of a certain field, kuge gave their disciples many licenses certifying that the disciples had learned a certain field and allowed them to perform in public or sometimes to teach others. Disciples were expected to pay their master a fee for each issued license. During the Edo period, this was an important source of income for the ''kuge''.
In 1869 during the [[Meiji Restoration]] the ''kuge'' merged with the daimyō to form a single aristocratic group, the ''[[kazoku]]''.
Others associated with the ''kuge'' included {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} priests, Kyoto cultural patrons, {{ill|Geisha|en|Geisha|qid=Q82723|lt=Geisha|1=Geisha}}, and actors.
==Classification== The ''kuge'' were divided into two classes, the higher ''dojo'' and the lower ''jige''. In the 12th century conventional differences were established among the ''dōjō'', separating them into groups according to their office at court. These determined the highest office to which they could be appointed. Within the ''dojo'' class, the groupings were: #{{nihongo||摂家|1=Five regent houses|1=Five regent houses|'''Sekke'''}}: could be appointed as [[Sesshō and Kampaku]]: This was the highest class of kuge. Only five families belonged to this class, all descended from {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|en|Fujiwara no Michinaga|qid=Q433133|lt=Fujiwara no Michinaga|1=Fujiwara no Michinaga}}. #{{nihongo||清華家|1=Seiga families|2=ja|3=清華家|'''Seiga families'''}}: could be appointed ''[[daijin]]'' (minister), including ''{{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|qid=Q1157586|lt=Daijō-daijin|1=Daijō-daijin}}'' (chancellor), the highest of the four ministers of the court. They were descended from the [[Fujiwara clan]] or [[Minamoto clan]], descendants of the emperors. #{{nihongo||大臣家|1=Daijinke|2=ja|3=大臣家|'''Daijinke'''}}: could be appointed ''{{ill|Naidaijin|en|Naidaijin|qid=Q1185338|lt=Naidaijin|1=Naidaijin}}'', if this office became vacant. In reality, the highest office they could normally achieve was ''{{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|qid=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon|1=Dainagon}}''. #{{nihongo||羽林家|1=Baronial Houses|1=Baronial Houses|2=ja|3=羽林家|'''Urinke'''}}: was a military class; they could be appointed ''dainagon'' or rarely to ''naidaijin''. #{{nihongo||名家|1=Mei families|2=ja|3=名家 (公家)|'''Meika'''|also pronounced "''Meike''"}}: was a civilian class; they could also be appointed ''dainagon''. #{{nihongo||半家|1=Half-house (Kuge)|2=ja|3=半家 (公家)|'''Hanke'''}}: was the lowest class among the dōjō, created in the late {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}. They could only be appointed to lower Ranks than ''{{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|qid=Q1133663|lt=sangi|1=Sangi (Japan)}}'' or ''{{ill|Chūnagon|en|Chūnagon|qid=Q1185382|lt=Chūnagon|1=Chūnagon}}''.
The ''jige'' class was associated with but not a part of the ''dojo'': *{{nihongo||地下家|1=Jigeke|2=ja|3=地下家|lt=Jigeke|'''Jigeke'''}}:This class is not a part of all the classes above. Their status is lower than Hanke and they may not enter certain important places in the imperial palace. They are in charge of miscellaneous business in the palace such as cooking and sanitary services. Like other Kuge classes, their positions and Ranks in the imperial court are hereditary. Most of the highest-classed kuge belonged to the [[Fujiwara clan]] and [[Minamoto clan]], but there were still other clans like the [[Sugawara clan]], the {{ill|Kiyohara clan|en|Kiyohara clan|qid=Q2975295|lt=Kiyohara clan|1=Kiyohara clan}}, and the [[Ōe clan]].
